# Grok-3, The Nvidia Shortcut, Competitive Implications (Stratechery Update 2025-2-18)

**Source**: inputs/saved_emails/Grok-3, The Nvidia Shortcut, Competitive Implications (Stratechery Update 2025-2-18)_Tue,_18_Feb_2025_13-17-33_+0000_195193525cf4bf71.eml
**Type**: email
**Created**: 2025-08-27T03:02:22.952683

---

Grok-3 appears to be state of the art; that speaks to the power of Nvidia, and actually increases the pressure to spend more.

View in browser ( https://stratechery.com/2025/grok-3-the-nvidia-shortcut-competitive-implications/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..V_N9uyzn6eCUQZ4Seij4AwSAqJ8QjHpT7HTf6Xz2mp6QmBEUWJYptjKLpNdrh6eORa1bWstUEI4pceirX0cj-hICn0jvso3sF8K8Vsw80iLWCG3btYxSGItYARorUoroaZyZu24WvNFG1CN9Agv89IBmMlVU1JO0BlZJWgfrEPQ-KXXdZu4lsWAMWB0cKSZ5GF39RodfU8kTPZGqOulUolSocBlk6H3KFsfxSPQ_jZhMxwqKtdmap5o6hUgYSyWdNgaLr6c_1rkxncD10LQagEcfDL_75valgi_yNKFyzAcTXW0-m25c8N4B8KXeTwB83w3pSIeKe0V3qRDp0VYssQ )

( https://stratechery.com )

Grok-3, The Nvidia Shortcut, Competitive Implications

( https://stratechery.com/2025/grok-3-the-nvidia-shortcut-competitive-implications/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..V_N9uyzn6eCUQZ4Seij4AwSAqJ8QjHpT7HTf6Xz2mp6QmBEUWJYptjKLpNdrh6eORa1bWstUEI4pceirX0cj-hICn0jvso3sF8K8Vsw80iLWCG3btYxSGItYARorUoroaZyZu24WvNFG1CN9Agv89IBmMlVU1JO0BlZJWgfrEPQ-KXXdZu4lsWAMWB0cKSZ5GF39RodfU8kTPZGqOulUolSocBlk6H3KFsfxSPQ_jZhMxwqKtdmap5o6hUgYSyWdNgaLr6c_1rkxncD10LQagEcfDL_75valgi_yNKFyzAcTXW0-m25c8N4B8KXeTwB83w3pSIeKe0V3qRDp0VYssQ ) Tuesday, February 18, 2025

Listen to this Update in your podcast player ( https://stratechery.passport.online/member/podcast?url=https%3A%2F%2Frss.stratechery.passport.online%2Ffeed%2Fpodcast%2F6EynvegSpNaDpnt4DJsHzD )

Good morning,

I hope you all had a pleasant President's Day holiday.

Andrew remains off this week after the birth of his second child; however, this week Bill Bishop and I will record a special Sharp Tech/Sharp China crossover episode.

Jon at Asianometry ( https://asianometry.passport.online/member ) , meanwhile, has a fun video about How Indonesian Instant Noodles Became a Nigerian Sensation ( https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qoLngV0pI2o ) , and a decidedly less fun video about Nissan Motors' Likely End ( https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qsL6JAUZFiQ ).

On to the Update:

Grok-3
------

From Bloomberg ( https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-02-18/musk-s-xai-debuts-grok-3-ai-bot-touting-benchmark-superiority ) :

Elon Musk’s artificial intelligence startup xAI showed off the updated Grok-3 model, showcasing a version of the chatbot technology that the billionaire has said is the “smartest AI on Earth.” Across math, science and coding benchmarks, Grok-3 beats Alphabet Inc.’s Google Gemini, DeepSeek’s V3 model, Anthropic’s Claude and OpenAI’s GPT- 4o , the company said via a live stream on Monday. Grok-3 has “more than 10 times” the compute power of its predecessor and completed pre-training in early January, Musk said in a presentation alongside three of xAI’s engineers. “We’re continually improving the models every day, and literally within 24 hours, you’ll see improvements,” Musk said.

The company introduced a new smart search engine with Grok-3 , calling it DeepSearch. DeepSearch is a reasoning chatbot that expresses its process of understanding a query and how it plans its response. It includes options for research, brainstorming and data analysis, the demonstration showed. Musk’s team also said it intends to release a voice-based chatbot “as soon as possible.”

Grok-3 appears to be one of if not the best-performing base model in the world; it is topping the usual benchmarks ( https://x.com/amasad/status/1891709057238507526?s=46 ) (but not if you include o3 ( https://x.com/12exyz/status/1891723056931827959 ) ), and tops all categories with the highest ever score in LMArena ( https://x.com/lmarena_ai/status/1891706264800936307 ) , an increasingly difficult feat ( https://x.com/GavinSBaker/status/1891723733976465420 ) as models have converged in the GPT-4 class. Andrew Karpathy is not an objective observer — he is the former Senior Director of AI at Tesla — but his review on X ( https://x.com/karpathy/status/1891720635363254772 ) is worth a read; some highlights:

The impression overall I got here is that this is somewhere around o1-pro capability, and ahead of DeepSeek-R1 , though of course we need actual, real evaluations to look at…

The impression I get of DeepSearch is that it's approximately around Perplexity DeepResearch offering (which is great!), but not yet at the level of OpenAI's recently released "Deep Research", which still feels more thorough and reliable…

As far as a quick vibe check over ~2 hours this morning, Grok 3 + Thinking feels somewhere around the state of the art territory of OpenAI's strongest models ( o1 -pro, $200/month), and slightly better than DeepSeek -R1 and Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking. Which is quite incredible considering that the team started from scratch ~1 year ago, this timescale to state of the art territory is unprecedented.

This gets at the first Grok-3 takeaway: it is simultaneously surprising and not surprising. Start with the latter: xAI famously built a 100,000 GPU data center in Memphis (and now has 200,000 ( https://x.com/NinaDSchick/status/1891711656234975410 ) GPUs), and used 20,000 GPUs to train Grok-3 ; that's the largest cluster ever and, sure enough, it resulted in a top tier model. That's not surprising given it is what scaling laws predicted, but it is comforting, as there was a question as to whether scaling laws had hit a wall.

What is surprising is that xAI is only 19 months old: the company has gone from incorporation to largest GPU cluster in the world to arguably the best model in less time than it has taken OpenAI to go from GPT-4 to GPT-5 (albeit with substantial updates along the way, including a big 4o update just this past weekend). That is certainly a testament to both Elon Musk and his team — just ask Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang ( https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lpLFjQ-bRv8 ) — and to the fact that not only are models commoditizing, but so is the knowledge of how and the wherewithal to build them.

The Nvidia Shortcut
-------------------

xAI's success is also a big win for Nvidia, and this story is in many respects the flipside of the DeepSeek one. I wrote in DeepSeek FAQ ( https://stratechery.com/2025/deepseek-faq/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..XZU9dHshwrDwnCgSwZ2xxIUDNDVkV57F_VgXIo5s_ZbELE-R6h1sSeFLe9ekljvuhEhjqxfme2NPFspV8-TDhxaR8P45y_KNl3_iDX8r3olzInn5ux6FqbH4Z1F-BrqZZ5TLCTjcdeshhb3aZqSj9od0rxSVWZppKg0KLVsNPLzdtsnrzSoRUaPctXNDazdJextEwg2MfQ8EK5NY5X8Ii1ltbQPHJTd3-dfnF9ATDNWmdwAte2r2Xn1lckbBJ2rWxn5SB0rzmInJ1XqWoeqezsTkvK7y7LQ7lQC6qVOHRzNbpBzbg6ugnfsqohd8K_Hu48yd7UltRUMYfWK7D4NPzw ) :

There are real challenges this news presents to the Nvidia story. Nvidia has two big moats:

* CUDA is the language of choice for anyone programming these models, and CUDA only works on Nvidia chips.
* Nvidia has a massive lead in terms of its ability to combine multiple chips together into one large virtual GPU.

These two moats work together. I noted above that if DeepSeek had access to H100s they probably would have used a larger cluster to train their model, simply because that would have been the easier option; the fact they didn’t, and were bandwidth constrained, drove a lot of their decisions in terms of both model architecture and their training infrastructure. Just look at the U.S. labs: they haven’t spent much time on optimization because Nvidia has been aggressively shipping ever more capable systems that accommodate their needs. The route of least resistance has simply been to pay Nvidia. DeepSeek, however, just demonstrated that another route is available: heavy optimization can produce remarkable results on weaker hardware and with lower memory bandwidth; simply paying Nvidia more isn’t the only way to make better models.

It remains, however, a very good way to make better models! The inverse of DeepSeek slaving away over optimizations for months or years is xAI simply buying the best possible chips complete with the best possible networking that makes a 20,000 GPU cluster usable in mere weeks or months, and training a better model.

Moreover, there is that bit I noted above about commoditization, not just in terms of models but also the knowledge of how to build these models; this isn't completely accurate — xAI has an excellent and experienced team — but I still think it's fair to say that Grok-3 reaffirms that the limiting factor in having a leading edge model is having access to Nvidia chips. In this respect DeepSeek is the exception that proves the rule: you can have a cracked team that works around things like the chip ban, but the best path to success continues to run through Nvidia.

Competitive Implications
------------------------

I did get access to Grok-3 (by being an X Premium Plus subscriber), but not the thinking and search modes; I also have to say that I find myself using Grok-2 quite a bit, almost entirely via its integration into the X app. It turns out being plugged into a pre-existing distribution channel is very useful!

I also never use it anywhere else; for me, product matters ( https://stratechery.com/2024/chatgpt-pro-o1-pro-sora/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..a3-OyiUKHNSnikad-PrCJyQEAskJjbjceSSdOQ-rPGqw-xcmAB0EpnqkA8ZdBFmn3ZKUN8oZE5uVTNkNBmKdrBq7REHs17XM-HgKBcKgocl8bY4qRddHttD2iw93q_qPvlwRb62FMUvoIcQYs597CmK0_SlrXLRprkrziVWLwfT64bGaB1Vv5rLAaS4arVsO2kx6Dn1AbIDnTgVIpDeo-9IcwaUMZSrdYfp5Jj00wsy85RrArfWp3qCYJljRTJWtPMcJ2VqklT4AI1n3tsba0qChCOsFqcWr_fTpETgA2raNkmOJj0DdGRCnOYUhdx0MkX3yuF4SvHfTVdZsN7TEvw ) , and ChatGPT remains my product of choice. I am also curious to what extent ChatGPT Deep Research's advantage over other competitors like Google, Perplexity, and now xAI are due simply to o3 still being the best model versus OpenAI spending more time crafting a better tool. Regardless, the impetus for OpenAI to focus on being a consumer tech company ( https://stratechery.com/2023/the-accidental-consumer-tech-company-chatgpt-meta-and-product-market-fit-aggregation-and-apis/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..dXXEgRahECrxBacWPKPvo6nG0_5Y6OFn-xA96WuLqBifVPN2Hdr4oaskC3T-l9WeeH7Rpo-w7hvssV321vziZs5RLf5DCBgB2FUqA4-vHTKDTG7I2zcybOM9xvX1dCskqniuaTlxUPqrUCOrPYw6tKWk50JVTAHKkZ7yWoKXkGuZPPhd-xEGbjfzAHp836JOCffAmFbXxvitYHJHFB1MLEQ5TXyXqj69vv_CkZedrQG783Ssay7hDHJL92pMcQ-a9q-dmxHDqU0nUqjHVqNl4O8BOeYAf2I4p4W5nk8zb9dy8v_KwzaQrnUmX1po_aEY07PlakV5TG8gksqpZ3LCHw ) is as strong as ever; OpenAI is the only AI lab to have organically built its own distribution channel (and they really, really need to get an advertising product out the door, to ensure they are serving free customers the best possible models).

This also explains why OpenAI is tying up with Oracle and Softbank ( https://stratechery.com/2025/stargate-the-end-of-microsoft-and-openai/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..V_4hrnrecnbX8kY_lP-UjfYxAGmPiC60bCyFseDn0Rytt0bh_u0RFRqLWuF-_Z-W-eDHbS4_UpGxhwJM1qSAcKvGVCyaKrhcoKPyruxD283X9_sZVFVfPSwK-tmfs4dxy_YCEoMhFLVGkNx1S5N8JKYN8E18Yj3T2zSUGMBdiNGrD7TLKAwaxeFlwBUNDR4N1EXOd2yqhnIpxgpHw_Mkeo7-mkUfjh8jy9QFnIDRWqFNnd_lzu9QvlQxrgs4glOexVG2l8RqYQRO7MftdAXWK1YsudIfMR6bYxpuCAU1Qlm3a4pFn8d5ljnYAMUUaLduSTTepttCQGZn9b4AEEYPPg ) and trying to become a for-profit; xAI raised $6 billion last November ( https://www.cnbc.com/2024/11/15/elon-musks-xai-raising-up-to-6-billion-to-purchase-100000-nvidia-chips-for-memphis-data-center.html ) and actually owns its own GPUs. Musk also said on the livestream that xAI is building out a 1 million GPU cluster ( https://x.com/SawyerMerritt/status/1891739471756001432 ) , although that's going to take quite a bit more money than even the $10 billion xAI is reportedly raising ( https://www.investopedia.com/elon-musk-xai-targets-usd75b-valuation-with-potential-usd10b-capital-raise-report-says-11680443 ).

xAI's success also helps explain this story from Bloomberg ( https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-02-17/openai-co-founder-s-startup-is-fundraising-at-a-30-billion-plus-valuation ) :

OpenAI co-founder Ilya Sutskever is raising more than $1 billion for his startup at a valuation of over $30 billion, according to a person familiar with the matter — vaulting the nascent venture into the ranks of the world’s most valuable private technology companies. Greenoaks Capital Partners, a San Francisco-based venture capital firm, is leading the deal for the startup, Safe Superintelligence, and plans to invest $500 million, said the person, who asked not to be identified discussing private information. Greenoaks is also an investor in AI companies Scale AI and Databricks Inc. The round marks a significant valuation jump from the $5 billion that Sutskever’s company was worth before, according to Reuters, which earlier reported some details of the new funding. The financing talks are ongoing and the details could still change.

First off, a disclaimer: Daniel Gross, a frequent Stratechery Interview guest ( https://stratechery.com/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..ArwqSJp7JGwFgpc944NEBCF1-ktfGPyq0fseOe859S254yscjuRtcpi9lJmCC6ppev5eEKWBfsXLQcitFLrgbDDe_WahzOYiR4ZKktUwg-g3KJRCpeKraB8b-V441LLm0xgyyIntRWA2i5EqX35A_rghX8Nq4o4LQI5bDk0rRouXBpQW-sn9pZ102s_k3IEmr6-yhoiI4_DUPR6Zs7RAhrlWddG4N4sc6Ro3Rg0pYPOWAEDJxFG0FsuFayegunP933QBgqadRf4125k-DEKYHDFgpeb6ucRet-8wai2VBAp3JQ8xLLWpgk8TmM5CdBvDLy7naKh_E9naVpO9LhxHww&s=daniel+gross+nat+friedman ) , is the CEO of SSI; that noted, I don't know anything about SSI beyond what is public, which is very little! To that end, how can a company that is less than a year old, which has pledged to not release a product until it has achieved safe superintelligence, raise so much money at such a steep valuation increase? Well, xAI explains why: it is absolutely viable to have started late and — provided you have the funds — catch up quickly, and of course SSI's talent speaks for itself.

SSI is also, I would imagine, a big Nvidia customer — again, I don't know that to be true, but what else might the money be going towards? — which brings this conversation back to chips. On one hand, chips remain the gating factor to the "easiest" route to a frontier model; on the other hand, this is why DeepSeek matters: they're not the only route to something that is at least competitive. The debate on the value of an attempted export ban remains an open one!

What does seem over is the idea of there being one model to rule them all, but for one fact: why else are all of these companies spending so wildly on the prize? The reason is the belief that there is a prize — AI that recursively improves itself — in which second place is the proverbial set of steak knives. And, given that Grok-3 reaffirms compute scaling, the willingness to spend in the face of yet another legitimate contender has probably only increased.

This Update will be available as a podcast later today. To receive it in your podcast player, visit Stratechery ( https://stratechery.passport.online/member ).

Thanks for being a subscriber, and have a great day!
